introduction: Follow these steps to design an ER diagram and map it to a relational schema.

steps.title: Identify the Problem Domain
steps.content:
Choose a real-world scenario (e.g., University Management System, Library Management System, 
        Hospital Management System). Clearly define the scope and requirements of the system.

steps.title: Identify Entities
steps.content:
List all entities in the system. Entities are real-world objects or concepts that can be 
        distinctly identified. Examples: Student, Course, Department, Employee, Book, etc.
subpoints: Write down all entities
Ensure each entity is distinct and meaningful

steps.title: Identify Attributes for Each Entity
steps.content:
For each entity, list all attributes that describe it. Classify attributes as:
        
        Example: Student entity with attributes: student_id (primary key), name, email, date_of_birth, address (composite).
subpoints: Simple attributes (atomic values)
Composite attributes (can be divided into sub-attributes)
Single-valued or multi-valued attributes
Derived attributes (calculated from other attributes)

steps.title: Identify Primary Keys
steps.content:
For each entity, identify the primary key attribute(s) that uniquely identify each instance. 
        Primary keys are underlined in ER diagrams.
steps.code:
Example:
Student: student_id (primary key)
Course: course_id (primary key)

steps.title: Identify Relationships
steps.content: Identify relationships between entities. Determine the type of relationship:
subpoints: One-to-One (1:1): Each instance of Entity A relates to at most one instance of Entity B
One-to-Many (1:N): One instance of Entity A relates to many instances of Entity B
Many-to-Many (M:N): Many instances of Entity A relate to many instances of Entity B
steps.code:
Example:
Student ENROLLS Course (M:N)
Department HAS Employee (1:N)

steps.title: Determine Cardinality and Participation
subpoints: Cardinality: The number of instances (1, N, M)
Participation: Total (double line) or Partial (single line)
steps.code:
Example:
Every employee MUST belong to a department (total participation)
Not every student enrolls in a course (partial participation)

steps.title: Identify Weak Entities (if any)
steps.content:
Identify entities that cannot be uniquely identified without a relationship to another entity. 
        Weak entities are represented by double rectangles and require an identifying relationship.
steps.code:
Example:
Dependant (weak entity) depends on Employee (strong entity)

steps.title: Draw the ER Diagram
steps.content: Using standard ER notation, draw the complete ER diagram:
subpoints: Rectangles for entities
Ovals for attributes
Diamonds for relationships
Double rectangles for weak entities
Underline primary keys
Indicate cardinality on relationship lines
Use double lines for total participation

steps.title: Map Strong Entities to Relations
steps.content: For each strong entity, create a relation (table):
subpoints: Entity name becomes table name
All attributes become columns
Primary key becomes the table's primary key
Composite attributes are flattened into separate columns
steps.code:
Example:
Entity: Student(student_id, name, email, DOB)
Relation: Student(student_id, name, email, date_of_birth)
          Primary Key: student_id

steps.title: Map Weak Entities to Relations
steps.content: For each weak entity, create a relation including:
subpoints: All attributes of the weak entity
Primary key of the owner entity as a foreign key
Combined primary key (owner's key + weak entity's partial key)
steps.code:
Example:
Weak Entity: Dependant(dep_name, age, relationship)
Owner Entity: Employee(emp_id, name)
Relation: Dependant(emp_id, dep_name, age, relationship)
          Primary Key: (emp_id, dep_name)
          Foreign Key: emp_id references Employee

steps.title: Map 1:1 Relationships
steps.content: For one-to-one relationships, include the foreign key in either of the participating entities:
steps.code:
Example:
Person(ssn, name) OWNS Passport(passport_no, country)
Option 1: Person(ssn, name, passport_no)
Option 2: Passport(passport_no, country, ssn)

steps.title: Map 1:N Relationships
steps.content: For one-to-many relationships, include the foreign key in the entity on the "many" side:
steps.code:
Example:
Department(dept_id, dept_name) HAS Employee(emp_id, name, salary)
Result: Employee(emp_id, name, salary, dept_id)
        Foreign Key: dept_id references Department

steps.title: Map M:N Relationships
steps.content: For many-to-many relationships, create a separate relation with foreign keys from both entities:
steps.code:
Example:
Student(student_id, name) ENROLLS Course(course_id, title)
Result: Enrollment(student_id, course_id, grade, semester)
        Primary Key: (student_id, course_id)
        Foreign Keys: student_id, course_id

steps.title: Handle Multi-valued Attributes
steps.content: For multi-valued attributes, create a separate relation:
steps.code:
Example:
Employee(emp_id, name) with multi-valued attribute: phone_numbers
Result: Employee(emp_id, name)
        Employee_Phone(emp_id, phone_number)
        Foreign Key: emp_id references Employee

steps.title: Verify the Relational Schema
steps.content: Review the mapped relational schema to ensure:
subpoints: All entities are mapped to relations
All relationships are properly represented
Primary keys and foreign keys are correctly identified
No information is lost in the mapping process

expectedOutcomes: Ability to identify entities, attributes, and relationships from a problem domain
Understanding of ER diagram notation and symbols
Knowledge of cardinality and participation constraints
Skill in mapping ER diagrams to normalized relational schemas
Understanding of how to handle weak entities, composite attributes, and multi-valued attributes
